title: [UI only] Open on a conversations list by default, not the unencrypted mesh (secure by default)
author: ypreiser
body:
**App version:** current `main` (checked July 2026)

UI-only change — just which screen the app opens on, nothing protocol-level.

### What I found

`ConversationStore.swift`, line 329:
```swift
@Published private(set) var activeChannel: ChannelID = .mesh
```
This is the default on every launch, including the first one. No onboarding redirects a new user elsewhere — checked for `firstLaunch`, `isFirstRun`, `onboarding`, found none.

### Why it matters

A new user lands straight in `#mesh` — unencrypted, visible to every device within 7 hops — before they understand what it is.
The whitepaper describes BitChat as built for secure, private communication. This default works against that in real-world use like protests and disasters.

### Suggested fix

- Open to a conversations list (private chats + joined channels) by default instead of `#mesh`.
- Keep `#mesh` easy to reach, just not automatic.
- **Add a clear warning that `#mesh` is unencrypted and public**, shown when a user opens it — not just a quieter default, but an explicit heads-up at the point of use.
- For a brand-new user with no chats or channels yet, don't just show an empty list — add a simple prompt to start a chat or browse channels.

Happy to PR if this direction sounds right.

title: [UI only] Verification badge missing from chat; local alias field exists but unused (secure by default)
author: ypreiser
body:
**App version:** current `main` (checked July 2026)

UI-only change, no protocol or core logic changes needed.

Following up on #42 and #370, closed as resolved against the current architecture. The verification system (`VerificationService`, `SecureIdentityStateManager`) exists now, but two gaps remain. Security features only help if they're easy to see and use — right now both of these are built but hidden.

---

### 1 — Verified badge not shown in chat

`isVerified` is only used in `FingerprintView.swift` and `VerificationViews.swift` — both profile screens. `ContentView.swift`, where messages actually render, never checks it. Users have to leave the chat to see if someone is verified. In practice, an impersonator looks identical to a real verified contact right in the conversation — the exact scenario verification is meant to prevent.

**Fix:** show a small badge next to the sender's name in chat messages and in peer list rows, using the existing `isVerified` value. Display-only change.

---

### 2 — Local alias field exists but nothing sets it

`IdentityModels.swift` already has:
```swift
var localPetname: String?   // User's name for this peer
```
Already read in `BLEService.swift`, `PrivateConversationModels.swift`, `VerificationModel.swift`, `ChatPeerIdentityCoordinator.swift` — but every place that creates a `SocialIdentity` passes `localPetname: nil`. No UI anywhere sets it.

**Fix:** add a way to rename a peer (long-press or edit icon) that saves through `SecureIdentityStateManager`. Read side is done, just needs a write path.

---

Both fit BitChat's secure-by-default goal: make the security that's already there visible and accessible, not opt-in and buried.

Happy to PR either part if welcome.

title: Received images unviewable in DM: render flat grey, tap-to-reveal doesn't work
author: jackjackbits
body:
**Observed on-device** (iOS 26.5 + macOS) during feature testing. Pre-existing (not tied to a specific feature).

In a DM, a **received** image renders as a **flat grey box with "tap to reveal"**, and tapping does nothing — the image is effectively unviewable.

**Ruled out via investigation:**
- **Data is intact.** The received file on disk is a valid, full-detail JPEG (e.g. 42 KB, 336×448, real content) at `…/Application Support/files/images/incoming/*.jpg`. Not a transport/storage bug.
- **Mask is correct.** `BlockRevealMask.path` at `fraction=1` adds all `columns×rows` blocks → full coverage (`BlockRevealImageView.swift`).
- **Received images get `fraction=1`.** `mediaSendState` returns `progress=nil` for non-sending (`MediaMessageView.swift`), so `revealProgress=nil` → `fraction=1`.
- The `"tap to reveal"` caption only renders when `platformImage != nil`, so the image **did load**.

**Conclusion:** the reveal **tap isn't firing** in the DM image view, so the image stays blurred (reads as flat grey) and can't be viewed. Render path: `MessageListView.messageRow` → `MediaMessageView` → `BlockRevealImageView`. The image's gesture is `TapGesture().simultaneously(with: swipeDrag)`. Leading suspects for the swallowed tap (needs the **Xcode View Debugger** to confirm which layer): the DM sheet's swipe-to-close `.highPriorityGesture(DragGesture)` on the ancestor (`ContentSheetViews.swift`), and/or the message-list stack (`ScrollView`+`LazyVStack`, per-row `.contentShape` + `.contextMenu`, list-level `.onTapGesture(count:3)`).

**Fix approach:** local repro in the simulator + View Debugger to inspect the gesture responder chain and confirm (breakpoint) whether the tap handler fires; then fix the swallowing layer (likely make reveal a `Button`, or drop the ancestor `highPriorityGesture`). Blind device rebuilds were not effective (and a stale-build issue masked results — verify any build actually deploys via `strings`/mtime).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

title: Peer-ID rotation leaves a ~1min ghost peer + drops directed packets on the other side
author: jackjackbits
body:
**Observed on-device** (3-node BLE mesh, iOS 26.5) during feature testing.

When a peer relaunches, it rotates its ephemeral peer ID (normal privacy behavior). The *other* side keeps a stale `peripheral → old-peerID` binding for ~45–60s (the reachability retention window), which causes two visible symptoms until the stale entry ages out:

1. A **duplicate/ghost peer** in the peer list (e.g. "2 alices / 3 peers" when there should be 2).
2. The spoofing guard **drops directed packets** to the retired ID: `BLEIngressPacketGuard` → `directSenderMismatch` at `BLEService.swift:1020` logs `🚫 SECURITY: Sender ID spoofing attempt detected! Peripheral … claimed to be <newID> but is bound to <oldID>` and returns nil (packet dropped). Retired-ID **public** messages are also dropped as unverifiable.

The peer is otherwise reachable (its re-announce + prekey bundle under the new ID are accepted), so mesh/broadcast still flows — it's specifically directed traffic on the stale-bound connection that's dropped. **Self-heals** after the retention window, but it's a ~1-minute window of a ghost peer + dropped directed traffic on every peer restart.

**Caveat / to confirm:** this may be partly a `devicectl`-relaunch artifact (the BLE connection may not have torn down the way a normal app-kill does). Please **retest with a clean force-quit** on a device to confirm it reproduces in normal use before treating as a definite bug. If it does reproduce, the fix is to refresh/rebind the peripheral→peerID binding on a legitimately re-announced (signed) peer rather than treating the rotation as a spoof.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

title: UX audit: a set of small, self-contained usability improvements — which would you take PRs for?
author: hotpixelgroup
body:
I did a focused UX pass over the iOS/macOS app and have a set of small, independently-reviewable improvements ready. Rather than open them all at once, I'd like your steer on which are welcome and how you'd prefer them scoped. Each is UI-layer only (no protocol/transport/crypto/message-semantics changes) and each can stand alone as its own small PR.

First concrete one is up as #1354 (a genuine bug: the command autocomplete suggests slash commands the processor rejects).

### Ready as small, low-risk PRs (bug fixes + accessibility)
- **Command suggestions** — suggests `/dm`, `/favorite` that the processor rejects → align aliases, fix gating, add `/help`. *(#1354)*
- **Shadowed fingerprint gesture** — the peer-row double-tap to view a fingerprint is registered after the single tap, so it never fires. One-line ordering fix.
- **Peer-list & delivery-status accessibility** — the reachability icons and delivery glyphs are unlabeled (and use macOS-only `.help` tooltips, inert on iOS); add VoiceOver labels reusing existing strings.
- **Jump-to-latest affordance** — when scrolled up, nothing signals new messages arrived; a small pill to jump back.
- **Localize two hardcoded failure reasons** (`"Not delivered"`, `"Encryption failed"`).

### Additive UX (slightly more opinionated)
- **Delivery status legibility** — tap-to-reveal the explanation on iOS, a visible reason + resend for failed DMs, a non-color cue for read vs delivered.
- **DM visual signature** — an open DM looks identical to the public room; a lock caption + channel-aware composer placeholder so it's unmistakable which context you're typing in.
- **Empty-state guidance** — the timeline with no peers is currently blank; add terminal-styled "what this is / waiting for peers" lines.
- **People-list actions** — mesh rows have no block/favorite/verify menu (they're slash-command-only); mirror the geohash list's context menu.

### Would want your input before writing (changes deliberate behavior)
- **Panic-wipe safety** — the triple-tap-logo instant wipe shares its tap target with single-tap app-info; an optional short "tap again to confirm" arming step keeps it fast under duress but avoids accidental wipes. I know this is behavior you actively curate, so I'd rather discuss than surprise-PR it.
- **First-launch Bluetooth priming** — a one-time intro before the permission prompt (which currently fires over a blank screen), which necessarily defers runtime start until the user taps through. Opinionated about launch flow, so flagging first.

If any of these are of interest I'll send them as separate small PRs matching the shape of #1354. Happy to drop any that don't fit the project's direction.

title: Unifi Firewall flags bitchat traffic as network intrusion attempt
author: LuisThe0ne
body: Hi there, I recently noticed a lot of network intrusion attempts on my Unifi Firewall. After digging deeper i found out that bitchat communicates with the IPs in question. Unifi flags them as "ET TOR Known Tor Relay/Router (Not Exit) Node Traffic group 339". Is this expected behaviour? And if, what is the TOR network used for by bitchat? Detailed Info: CEF:0|Ubiquiti|UniFi Network|10.4.57|201|Threat Detected and Blocked|7|UNIFIcategory=Security UNIFIsite=< MASKED > UNIFIhost=< MASKED > proto=TCP spt=443 dpt=47526 act=blocked app=Other UNIFIrisk=medium UNIFIpolicyName=TOR UNIFIpolicyType=IDS/IPS UNIFIdirection=incoming deviceInboundInterface=Default deviceOutboundInterface=Internet 1 UNIFIdeviceMac=xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx UNIFIdeviceName=< MASKED > UNIFIdeviceModel=UDM-Pro UNIFIdeviceIp=< MASKED > UNIFIdeviceVersion=5.1.15 src=192.42.116.137 dst=192.168.0.107 UNIFIsrcRegion=NL UNIFIdstRegion=NL UNIFItotalBytes=12030 UNIFItotalPackets=31 UNIFIpacketsReceived=13 UNIFIpacketsSent=18 UNIFIbytesReceived=2593 UNIFIbytesSent=9437 UNIFIflowDuration=5m 52s UNIFIflowCount=2 UNIFIflowId=null UNIFIflowEndTime=Jun 17, 2026 at 4:20:39.283 AM UNIFIflowStartTime=Jun 17, 2026 at 4:14:46.382 AM UNIFIipsSessionId=1949968891303007 UNIFIipsSignature=ET TOR Known Tor Relay/Router (Not Exit) Node Traffic group 339 UNIFIipsSignatureId=2522338 UNIFIutcTime=2026-06-17T02:14:47.863Z msg=A network intrusion attempt from 192.42.116.137 to 192.168.0.107 has been detected and blocked.

title: Bug: Excessive data usage
author: dmcarrington
body: I've had Bitchat running on my Android phone for just a couple of days while out of the house, and it has somehow chewed through 2GB+ of mobile data. Not actively used it during this period, just sent a couple of test message previously while on my home wifi

title: Feature Request: Windows client support
author: Greendino125
body:
Summary
There is currently no Windows client for bitchat. This limits adoption for users who rely on Windows as their primary desktop OS and want to participate in the mesh network without needing an Apple device.
Current state
Bitchat is available on iOS and macOS. The codebase appears to be Swift-based, which ties it tightly to Apple platforms.
Request
Add a Windows-compatible client, either as:

A native port using a cross-platform language (e.g. Rust, Go, or Flutter), or
A standalone Windows application that implements the same Bluetooth LE mesh protocol

Why this matters

Windows holds the majority desktop market share globally
Users without Apple hardware are fully excluded from the network, which weakens the mesh
A cross-platform implementation would also make future Linux support more feasible

Notes

Windows has Bluetooth LE support via the Windows.Devices.Bluetooth API (available from Windows 10 v1703+)
A protocol spec or documentation of the mesh layer would help third-party contributors attempt a Windows client independently
Happy to help test on Windows 10/11 if development starts

title: Test Audit
author: Sbauermaner

title: Request for Pre-compiled macOS (.dmg) Binary for Sideloading
author: mrtnd
body:
## Description
Currently, the [Bitchat releases page](https://github.com/permissionlesstech/bitchat/releases) only provides the source code. While Bitchat is a decentralized communication tool, it currently lacks a pre-compiled desktop binary for macOS users.

In contrast, the [Bitchat Android repository](https://github.com/permissionlesstech/bitchat-android/releases) provides direct `.apk` files, allowing users to install the application without relying on a centralized storefront.

## Purpose
Providing a `.dmg` file (or a similar standalone binary) is essential for several reasons:

1.  **Sideloading & Autonomy:** Many users prefer or require sideloading to maintain control over their hardware without the intervention of centralized gatekeepers.
2.  **Bypassing Restricted App Stores:** In certain jurisdictions, regulated app stores may restrict or delist privacy-focused tools. Direct access to binaries ensures that the software remains available to users in restricted regions.
3.  **Accessibility:** Compiling from source is a barrier to entry for non-technical users who still require the privacy and decentralization benefits of Bitchat.

## Request
Would it be possible to include a `.dmg` file in the GitHub Releases alongside the source code? This would bring the desktop distribution in line with the accessibility and anti-censorship standards established by the Android version.

Thank you for your work on this project!

title: Proposal: SDK packages for the BitChat protocol
author: SwarnavaB
body:
## What

I've built and published a set of SDK packages that implement the BitChat binary
protocol and Nostr transport layer:

### npm ([protocol-core](https://github.com/bitchat-sdk/protocol-core-js) · [nostr](https://github.com/bitchat-sdk/nostr-js))
```sh
npm install @bitchat-sdk/protocol-core  # Binary encode/decode, TLV codec, peer ID utils
npm install @bitchat-sdk/nostr           # Nostr relay client, NIP-17 gift wrap, packet embedding
```

### PyPI ([protocol-core](https://github.com/bitchat-sdk/protocol-core-py) · [nostr](https://github.com/bitchat-sdk/nostr-py))
```sh
pip install bitchat-protocol  # Binary encode/decode, TLV codec, peer ID utils
pip install bitchat-nostr     # Nostr relay client, NIP-17 gift wrap, packet embedding
```

### Swift Package Manager ([BitchatProtocol](https://github.com/bitchat-sdk/BitchatProtocol) · [BitchatNostr](https://github.com/bitchat-sdk/BitchatNostr))
```swift
.package(url: "https://github.com/bitchat-sdk/BitchatProtocol", from: "0.1.0")  // Binary codec
.package(url: "https://github.com/bitchat-sdk/BitchatNostr", from: "0.1.0")     // Nostr integration
```

All packages are wire-compatible with the current BitChat iOS and Android apps.
They're tested against a shared golden vector fixture suite that verifies
encode/decode parity across all three languages.

## What's included

- Protocol v1 and v2 header support
- Compression (raw deflate) with bomb detection
- TLV codec for AnnouncementPacket and PrivateMessagePacket
- NIP-17 gift wrap (seal → wrap → send, receive → unwrap → unseal)
- Relay client with reconnect, observability hooks, and metrics counters
- 100+ tests per language (unit, fixtures, fuzz)
- Cross-language golden vector fixtures
- CI workflow (GitHub Actions) for JS (Node 18/20/22), Python (3.10-3.12), Swift (5.9/5.10)

## What I'm proposing

I'd like to contribute these as an official part of the BitChat project — either as:

1. **A directory in this repo** (e.g. `ecosystem/packages/`) with its own CI job, or
2. **A separate repo** under the permissionlesstech org (e.g. `permissionlesstech/bitchat-sdk`)

Happy to do either. The packages are self-contained, don't touch any existing code,
and I'll maintain them going forward (tracking protocol changes and publishing updates).

Open to feedback on the approach before I put up a PR.

title: Voice record doesn't work
author: Rodping
body:
Hello, When I was chatting and tried to record a voice message to my friend, it didn't work. More precisely, when I recorded it and it was sent, my friend didn't receive anything even though I was right next to him. I know it's just a beta version, but I wanted to point it out anyway and let everyone know, otherwise everything is great so far. 

__Example:__
https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/7f8460fd-4722-4ace-951a-068bf26c1059

If it works and if I made any mistakes, please explain, thank you.
